title: The Uhlmann phase of Higher-Order Topological Insulators at Finite Temperature
abstract: We have studied the finite-temperature topology of higher-order topological insulators (HOTIs) based on the Uhlmann phase, which is a phase angle of the Uhlmann overlap. As an example of HOTIs, the Hamiltonian of the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model is constructed from Gamma matrices satisfying the Clifford algebra. This specific algebraic structure underpins the model's higher-order topological properties, including the quantization of the Uhlmann phase to $0$ or $π$. This quantization enables us to treat the abrupt jumps of the Uhlmann phase as an indication of the nontrivial topological phase of the BBH model at finite temperature. From the disappearance of these jumps, we determine the critical temperature at which the topological transition occurs. For a special choice of parameters, the Uhlmann overlap and the critical temperature can be computed analytically.
summary: This work develops the Uhlmann phase as a robust topological invariant for Higher-Order Topological Insulators (HOTIs) at finite temperatures. By analyzing the BBH model, the authors show that the topological phase is characterized by the quantization of this phase, and the transition to a trivial phase is precisely located by observing where these quantized jumps vanish at a critical temperature $T_c$.
main_problem: The study focuses on characterizing the topological phase of Higher-Order Topological Insulators (HOTIs) when the system is subjected to finite temperatures.
main_result: The Uhlmann phase serves as a finite-temperature topological index, where the topological transition is marked by the disappearance of abrupt jumps in this phase at a critical temperature ($T_c$).
method: The analysis generalizes the topological concept from zero temperature (using the Berry connection) to finite temperature by employing the Uhlmann connection and calculating the Uhlmann phase from the density matrix.
model_or_system: The primary model is the Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes (BBH) model, a 2D tight-binding Hamiltonian. The analysis involves calculating the density matrix $ho$ using Boltzmann weights at finite temperature $T$.
key_observables: The Uhlmann phase ($\Phi_U$), which is quantized to 0 or $\pi$ in the topological phase, and the critical temperature ($T_c$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), the parameter $m$ in the Hamiltonian, and the lattice momenta ($k_x, k_y$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the Uhlmann phase correctly captures the topological invariant at finite temperatures, and approximations are made regarding the dominant components of the density matrix at low temperatures.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Wannier centers of the BBH model and plot the Uhlmann phase ($\Phi_U$) or the Uhlmann overlap ($	ext{Tr}(ho_0 \mathcal{W}_U)$) as a function of temperature or momentum, demonstrating quantization or continuous variation.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the BBH model and its zero-temperature topology using nested Wilson loops. It then introduces the Uhlmann connection and phase to generalize this concept to finite temperature, deriving the critical temperature $T_c$ from the behavior of the Uhlmann phase.
why_interesting: The extension of topological invariants from ground-state properties (like Berry curvature) to finite-temperature mixed states using quantum information concepts (Uhlmann phase) provides a powerful, generalizable tool for studying thermal phase transitions in quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
